summary: Introduces 'saber' (to know), 'sí' (yes/if), 'explicar' (to explain), and practices building complex multi-clause sentences. Emphasizes confidence in attempting sentences block by block.

teachings.summary: Saber means 'to know'. It's an -er verb. Related to English 'savvy' and 'Homo sapien'. The -o → -e pattern for he/she/it applies: quiero/quiere, so saber follows similar patterns.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no quiere saber
examples.english: I want to know why he doesn't want to know

teachings.notes: Memory hooks: 'savvy' in British slang, 'Homo sapien' = the wise creature.

teachings.summary: Sí means 'yes' and si (same word) also means 'if'.

examples.english: I want to know if you want to know
I want to know if he wants to invite me

teachings.summary: Explanation in Spanish is 'explicación', not 'explanación'. This is an exception. English also has this root: 'inexplicable'.
teachings.rule: Explanation = explicación (exception to normal pattern). To explain = explicar.

examples.english: I want to explain something to you

examples.spanish: No intento justificarme, pero quiero explicarte algo
examples.english: I'm not trying to justify myself, but I want to explain something to you

teachings.notes: English has both roots: 'explain' and 'inexplicable' — the c-sound root is the one Spanish uses.

teachings.summary: Complex sentences should be built block by block, giving 100% mental energy to each block then forgetting it. This makes seemingly impossible sentences achievable.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no quiere venir a la casa a visitarme
examples.english: I want to know why she doesn't want to come to the house to visit me

teachings.notes: Divide up piece by piece. Once you've said a block, you don't need to hold it.

teachings.summary: Continued practice of the 'a' connecting verbs of movement to following infinitives, even when other words intervene.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber si quiere venir a la casa a visitarme
examples.english: I want to know if she wants to come to the house to visit me

teachings.notes: Even with 'a la casa' in between, the 'a' before 'visitarme' is still required because of 'venir'.
